Chem 3240 · Quantum Mechanics
Quantum Mechanics for Chemistry
Chapter 1: Intro to Quantum Mechanics
Chapter 2: Waves and the Wave Equation
2. Waves and wave equationWavesWave equationDEMO: Visualizing wavesDEMO: Bonus challenge
Made with MyST

Wave equation

What you will learn

  • Wave Equation Overview: The wave equation, a partial differential equation (PDE), fully describes the spatial and temporal evolution of waves.

  • Linearity of the Wave Equation: The wave equation is linear, meaning that any linear combination of solutions is also a solution. This allows the most general solution to be expressed as a linear combination of all possible solutions.

  • Boundary and Initial Conditions: To solve the wave equation for a specific physical system, boundary conditions must be specified. These include the values the wave function takes at the physical boundaries (e.g., x=0x = 0x=0 and x=Lx = Lx=L). Initial conditions with respect to time (e.g., at t=0t = 0t=0) may also be required.

  • Solution Method for 1D Guitar String: For a 1D guitar string, the wave equation is solved by separating variables, solving the resulting ordinary differential equations, and then applying boundary conditions at the two ends of the string.

  • Periodic Solutions: The solution for a 1D guitar string yields an infinite number of periodic solutions, parameterized by an integer nnn.

Classical Wave equation¶

  • The wave equation is an example of a second-order PDE (partial differential equation). This PDE governs the behavior of the displacement u(x,t)u(x,t)u(x,t) in time and space.

∂2u(x,t)∂x2=1v2∂2u(x,t)∂t2\frac{\partial^2 u(x,t)}{\partial x^2 }= \frac{1}{v^2}\frac{\partial^2 u(x,t)}{\partial t^2}∂x2∂2u(x,t)​=v21​∂t2∂2u(x,t)​
(1)

  • Applications of the Classical Wave Equation: To illustrate the applications of the classical wave equation, we will solve it for a 1D guitar string. This provides a comprehensive mathematical description of the string’s behavior.

  • Predicting Evolution: By specifying arbitrary initial conditions, the wave equation allows us to precisely predict the evolution of the string over time and space.

Solving Wave Equation: The big picture¶

  • 1. Boundary Conditions: To solve the wave equation for a specific physical situation, such as a guitar string fixed at both ends, we need to specify two boundary conditions. Mathematically, these are:

u(0,t)=0andu(L,t)=0u(0, t) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad u(L, t) = 0u(0,t)=0andu(L,t)=0
(2)

  • where u(x,t)u(x, t)u(x,t) represents the displacement of the string at position xxx and time ttt.

  • 2. Separation of Variables: A common method to solve such equations is the technique of separation of variables. This technique assumes that xxx and ttt vary independently of each other, allowing us to express u(x,t)u(x, t)u(x,t) as a product of two functions, each depending on only one variable. This lets us decompose the PDE into ordinary differential equations (ODEs).

u(x,t)=X(x)⋅T(t)u(x, t) = X(x) \cdot T(t)u(x,t)=X(x)⋅T(t)
(3)

  • 3. Principle of superposition: The wave equation is linear, which you can see from the uuu terms appearing to first order on both sides. Linearity means that a linear combination of two solutions u1u_1u1​ and u2u_2u2​ is also a solution. If you have nnn particular solutions, then you write the general solution as a linear combination of those nnn terms.

u=c1u1+c2u2u = c_1 u_1+c_2u_2u=c1​u1​+c2​u2​
(4)

Step 1: Plug the Product of Univariate Functions into the Wave Equation¶

  • Start with the 1D wave equation that can describe 1D guitar string:

1v2∂2u(x,t)∂t2=∂2u(x,t)∂x2\frac{1}{v^2}\frac{\partial^2 u(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = \frac{\partial^2 u(x,t)}{\partial x^2}v21​∂t2∂2u(x,t)​=∂x2∂2u(x,t)​
(5)

  • Substitute u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)u(x, t) = X(x)T(t)u(x,t)=X(x)T(t) into the wave equation:

1v2∂2X(x)T(t)∂t2=∂2X(x)T(t)∂x2\frac{1}{v^2}\frac{\partial^2 X(x)T(t)}{\partial t^2} = \frac{\partial^2 X(x)T(t)}{\partial x^2}v21​∂t2∂2X(x)T(t)​=∂x2∂2X(x)T(t)​
(6)

  • After rearranging, we get:

X(x)v2∂2T(t)∂t2=T(t)∂2X(x)∂x2\frac{X(x)}{v^2}\frac{\partial^2 T(t)}{\partial t^2} = T(t)\frac{\partial^2 X(x)}{\partial x^2}v2X(x)​∂t2∂2T(t)​=T(t)∂x2∂2X(x)​
(7)

  • Thus, we obtain two separate ordinary differential equations (ODEs) from the original partial differential equation (PDE):

1T(t)v2∂2T(t)∂t2=1X(x)∂2X(x)∂x2=K\frac{1}{T(t)v^2}\frac{\partial^2 T(t)}{\partial t^2} = \frac{1}{X(x)}\frac{\partial^2 X(x)}{\partial x^2} = KT(t)v21​∂t2∂2T(t)​=X(x)1​∂x2∂2X(x)​=K
(8)

  • where KKK is a constant.

Step 2: Solving Each Ordinary Differential Equation¶

After decomposing the PDE into ODEs, we solve each ODE separately:

∂2T(t)∂t2−KT(t)v2=0\frac{\partial^2 T(t)}{\partial t^2} - K T(t) v^2 = 0∂t2∂2T(t)​−KT(t)v2=0
(9)
∂2X(x)∂x2−KX(x)=0\frac{\partial^2 X(x)}{\partial x^2} - K X(x) = 0∂x2∂2X(x)​−KX(x)=0
(10)
Solving simple linear and homogeneous ODEs

Going from ODE to algebraic equations

  • To solve linear (no powers of yyy) and homogeneous (no constants, right-hand side is zero) ODEs, we plug in an exponential function, e.g. y(x)→ekxy(x) \rightarrow e^{kx}y(x)→ekx.

  • This turns the ODE into a simple algebraic equation in which the only variable is kkk.

  • In our case of the second-order wave equation, we will be dealing with simple quadratic equations:

ay′′+by′+cy=0a y^{''} + by^{'} + cy = 0ay′′+by′+cy=0
(11)
ak2+bk+c=0,ak^2 + bk + c = 0,ak2+bk+c=0,
(12)

The solutions of which are given as:

k1,2=−b±b2−4ac2a.k_{1,2} = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}.k1,2​=2a−b±b2−4ac​​.
(13)

  • The most general solution is then written as a linear combination. We need extra information to determine c1c_1c1​ and c2c_2c2​ (e.g. boundary or initial conditions).

y=c1ek1+c2ek2y = c_1e^{k_1}+c_2 e^{k_2}y=c1​ek1​+c2​ek2​
(14)

Example ODE

d2ydx2+4y=0\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} +4y = 0dx2d2y​+4y=0
(15)

  • For instance, if we plug y=ekxy=e^{kx}y=ekx into the following equation and cancel the exponentials on both sides, we get an algebraic equation in terms of kkk.

k2+4=0k^2 +4 = 0k2+4=0
(16)

  • We get two solutions (which could be complex, real, or repeated), which we then use to write the general solution in the form of a superposition.

k=±2ik=\pm 2ik=±2i
(17)
y=c1e+2i+c2e−2iy=c_1e^{+2i} + c_2e^{-2i}y=c1​e+2i+c2​e−2i
(18)
Solving the spatial part: when K>0K > 0K>0

  • If K>0K > 0K>0, we specify K=β2K = \beta^2K=β2, where β\betaβ is a constant. The general solution for X(x)X(x)X(x) is represented as a linear combination of particular solutions (principle of superposition):

    X(x)=c1eβx+c2e−βxX(x) = c_1 e^{\beta x} + c_2 e^{-\beta x}X(x)=c1​eβx+c2​e−βx
    (19)

    Applying the boundary conditions X(0)=X(L)=0X(0) = X(L) = 0X(0)=X(L)=0 leads to c1=c2=0c_1 = c_2 = 0c1​=c2​=0. Since a non-trivial linear combination of two exponential functions cannot be zero everywhere, this results in the trivial solution:

    X(x)=0X(x) = 0X(x)=0
    (20)

    This implies that the string does not move, so there is no music!

Solving the spatial part: when K<0K < 0K<0

  • If K<0K < 0K<0, we specify K=−β2K = -\beta^2K=−β2, where β\betaβ is a constant. The general solution for X(x)X(x)X(x) is:

    X(x)=c1eiβx+c2e−iβxX(x) = c_1 e^{i \beta x} + c_2 e^{-i \beta x}X(x)=c1​eiβx+c2​e−iβx
    (21)

    Using Euler’s formula, this can be rewritten as:

    X(x)=c1(cos⁡(βx)+isin⁡(βx))+c2(cos⁡(βx)−isin⁡(βx))X(x) = c_1 (\cos(\beta x) + i \sin(\beta x)) + c_2 (\cos(\beta x) - i \sin(\beta x))X(x)=c1​(cos(βx)+isin(βx))+c2​(cos(βx)−isin(βx))
    (22)

    Simplifying further:

    X(x)=(c1+ic2)cos⁡(βx)+(c1−ic2)sin⁡(βx)X(x) = (c_1 + i c_2) \cos(\beta x) + (c_1 - i c_2) \sin(\beta x)X(x)=(c1​+ic2​)cos(βx)+(c1​−ic2​)sin(βx)
    (23)

    Let A=c1+ic2A = c_1 + i c_2A=c1​+ic2​ and B=c1−ic2B = c_1 - i c_2B=c1​−ic2​:

    X(x)=Acos⁡(βx)+Bsin⁡(βx)X(x) = A \cos(\beta x) + B \sin(\beta x)X(x)=Acos(βx)+Bsin(βx)
    (24)

    Applying the boundary conditions X(0)=0X(0) = 0X(0)=0 and X(L)=0X(L) = 0X(L)=0:

    • At x=0x = 0x=0: A⋅1+B⋅0=0A \cdot 1 + B \cdot 0 = 0A⋅1+B⋅0=0, so A=0A = 0A=0.

    • At x=Lx = Lx=L: X(L)=Bsin⁡(βL)=0X(L) = B \sin(\beta L) = 0X(L)=Bsin(βL)=0.

    For a non-trivial solution, sin⁡(βL)=0\sin(\beta L) = 0sin(βL)=0, which implies:

    βL=nπwheren=1,2,3,…\beta L = n \pi \quad \text{where} \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsβL=nπwheren=1,2,3,…
    (25)

    Hence:

    β=nπL\beta = \frac{n \pi}{L}β=Lnπ​
    (26)
    X(x)=Bsin⁡(nπLx)X(x) = B \sin \left(\frac{n \pi}{L} x \right)X(x)=Bsin(Lnπ​x)
    (27)
Solving temporal part

  • Having identified the K<0K<0K<0 condition through the solution of the spatial part, we can now solve for T(t)T(t)T(t):

∂2T(t)∂t2−Kv2T(t)=0withK=β2\frac{\partial^2 T(t)}{\partial t^2} - K v^2 T(t) = 0 \quad \text{with} \quad K = \beta^2∂t2∂2T(t)​−Kv2T(t)=0withK=β2
(28)

  • Substitute K=β2K = \beta^2K=β2:

∂2T(t)∂t2−β2v2T(t)=0\frac{\partial^2 T(t)}{\partial t^2} - \beta^2 v^2 T(t) = 0∂t2∂2T(t)​−β2v2T(t)=0
(29)

  • The solution is:

T(t)=Dncos⁡(ωnt)+Ensin⁡(ωnt)T(t) = D_n \cos(\omega_n t) + E_n \sin(\omega_n t)T(t)=Dn​cos(ωn​t)+En​sin(ωn​t)
(30)
ωn=βv=nπvL\omega_n = \beta v = \frac{n \pi v}{L}ωn​=βv=Lnπv​
(31)

Solution for spatial part¶

  • Because of the boundary conditions (X(x)=X(L)=0X(x)=X(L)=0X(x)=X(L)=0) imposed at the ends of the guitar string, we found an infinite number of solutions indexed by an integer nnn. These terms are called normal modes and are visualized below.

X(x)=Bsin⁡(nπLx)X(x) = B \sin \left(\frac{n \pi}{L} x \right)X(x)=Bsin(Lnπ​x)
(32)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Parameters
L = 1.0
B = 1.0
ns = [1, 2, 3, 4]
x = np.linspace(0, L, 800)

# 2x2 subplot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8, 6))

for ax, n in zip(axes.ravel(), ns):
    Xn = B * np.sin(n * np.pi * x / L)
    ax.plot(x, Xn, color="C0")
    ax.axhline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
    ax.set_title(f"n = {n}")
    ax.set_xlabel("x")
    ax.set_ylabel("X(x)")
    ax.set_xlim(0, L)

fig.suptitle("Normal modes of a fixed–fixed string")
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96])

Solution for temporal part¶

  • For the temporal part, we do not have boundary conditions! Time is allowed to march forward into infinity. We find the solution in the form of a linear combination of sine and cosine functions with ωn=βv=nπvL\omega_n = \beta v = \frac{n \pi v}{L}ωn​=βv=Lnπv​, where n=1,2,3,…n = 1, 2, 3, \ldotsn=1,2,3,… indexes the normal modes.

T(t)=Dncos⁡(ωnt)+Ensin⁡(ωnt)=Ancos⁡(ωnt+ϕn)T(t) = D_n \cos(\omega_n t) + E_n \sin(\omega_n t) = A_n \cos (\omega_n t + \phi_n)T(t)=Dn​cos(ωn​t)+En​sin(ωn​t)=An​cos(ωn​t+ϕn​)
(33)

  • In the second line we use a trig identity to express the sum of cosine and sine in terms of a single cosine. We still have two constants to determine.

  • The constants AnA_nAn​ and ϕn\phi_nϕn​ in the temporal part are determined by initial conditions, e.g. what the amplitude and phase of the wave should be at the starting time t=0t=0t=0.

Step 3 Full solution: A linear combination of normal modes¶

  • After solving the ODEs for the spatial and temporal parts, we now combine them into a full solution.

  • The complete description of any vibrational motion of the guitar string is given by the sum of the normal modes, Xn(x)X_n(x)Xn​(x).

  • While the terms involving time, Tn(t)T_n(t)Tn​(t), depend on how and where the string is plucked, the normal modes Xn(x)X_n(x)Xn​(x) remain the same for a given string.

Important
u(x,t)=∑nXn(x)Tn(t)u(x, t) = \sum_n X_n(x) T_n(t)u(x,t)=n∑​Xn​(x)Tn​(t)
(34)
u(x,t)=∑nAnsin⁡(nπLx)⋅cos⁡(ωnt+ϕn)u(x, t) = \sum_n A_n \sin \left(\frac{n \pi}{L} x \right) \cdot \cos (\omega_n t + \phi_n)u(x,t)=n∑​An​sin(Lnπ​x)⋅cos(ωn​t+ϕn​)
(35)

  • xxx spatial profile of wave bounded in region [0,L][0, L][0,L]

  • ttt time variable is free (0,∞(0, \infty(0,∞)

  • n=0,1,2,n=0,1,2,n=0,1,2, index of normal mode

  • AnA_nAn​ and ϕn\phi_nϕn​ coefficients specified as part of initial conditions

About nodes¶

Places where the guitar string stays at the resting position of 0 are called nodes. We notice that the number of nodal points increases with nnn.

  • For n=1n = 1n=1: There are 0 nodes (excluding the endpoints). This is called the fundamental frequency or first harmonic.

  • For n=2n = 2n=2: There is 1 node. This is known as the first overtone or second harmonic.

  • For n=3n = 3n=3: There are 2 nodes. This is called the second overtone or third harmonic.

  • Note that the general solution to wave equation is expressed as a linear combination of all normal modes

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.animation import FuncAnimation
from IPython.display import HTML

# Parameters (reduced frames for faster export)
L = 1.0
v = 1.0
B = 1.0
A = {1: 1.0, 2: 1.0, 3: 1.0}
phi = {1: 0.0, 2: 0.0, 3: 0.0}

x = np.linspace(0, L, 600)

def X(n, x):
    return np.sin(n * np.pi * x / L)

def T(n, t):
    omega_n = n * np.pi * v / L
    return np.cos(omega_n * t + phi[n])

def u_mode(n, x, t):
    return B * X(n, x) * T(n, t)

def u_sum(modes, x, t):
    s = np.zeros_like(x)
    for n in modes:
        s += A[n] * X(n, x) * T(n, t)
    return s

omega1 = np.pi * v / L
T1 = 2 * np.pi / omega1
tmax = 2 * T1
frames = 120  # fewer frames
ts = np.linspace(0, tmax, frames)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8, 6))
axes = axes.ravel()
titles = ["n = 1", "n = 3", "n = 1 + 2", "n = 1 + 2 + 3"]
lines = []
for ax, title in zip(axes, titles):
    ax.set_xlim(0, L)
    ax.set_ylim(-1.6, 1.6)
    ax.set_xlabel("x")
    ax.set_ylabel("u(x,t)")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.axhline(0, linewidth=0.8)
    line, = ax.plot([], [])
    lines.append(line)

fig.suptitle("Standing waves on a fixed–fixed string")

def init():
    for line in lines:
        line.set_data([], [])
    return lines

def update(idx):
    t = ts[idx]
    lines[0].set_data(x, u_mode(1, x, t))
    lines[1].set_data(x, u_mode(3, x, t))
    lines[2].set_data(x, u_sum([1, 2], x, t))
    lines[3].set_data(x, u_sum([1, 2, 3], x, t))
    return lines

ani = FuncAnimation(fig, update, frames=frames, init_func=init, blit=True, interval=1000*(tmax/frames))

plt.close(fig)  # Prevents static display of the last frame
HTML(ani.to_jshtml())

2D Membrane Vibrations¶

  • The wave function of a 2D membrane with fixed edges depends on two independent spatial variables, xxx and yyy. By applying the method of separation of variables, we decompose the wave function into three ordinary differential equations.

u(x,y,t)=X(x)Y(y)T(t)u(x, y, t) = X(x) Y(y) T(t)u(x,y,t)=X(x)Y(y)T(t)
(36)

  • For the spatial components X(x)X(x)X(x) and Y(y)Y(y)Y(y), there are boundary conditions that must be satisfied at the fixed edges: Boundary condition along the XXX-axis: X(0)=X(L)=0X(0) = X(L) = 0X(0)=X(L)=0 and Boundary condition along the YYY-axis: Y(0)=Y(L)=0Y(0) = Y(L) = 0Y(0)=Y(L)=0

  • The 2D normal mode is the product of two 1D modes. Since the dimensions are independent, we get integers nnn for the xxx-direction and mmm for the yyy-direction:

u(x,y,t)=∑n∑mAnmcos⁡(ωnmt+ϕnm)sin⁡(nπxa)sin⁡(mπyb)u(x, y, t) = \sum_n \sum_m A_{nm} \cos(\omega_{nm}t + \phi_{nm}) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\left(\frac{m\pi y}{b}\right)u(x,y,t)=n∑​m∑​Anm​cos(ωnm​t+ϕnm​)sin(anπx​)sin(bmπy​)
(37)

  • The angular frequency ωnm\omega_{nm}ωnm​ depends on the geometry of the membrane (with dimensions aaa and bbb) and the mode numbers nnn and mmm:

ωnm=vπ(n2a2+m2b2)1/2\omega_{nm} = v\pi \left(\frac{n^2}{a^2} + \frac{m^2}{b^2}\right)^{1/2}ωnm​=vπ(a2n2​+b2m2​)1/2
(38)

Where:

  • vvv is the wave speed,

  • nnn and mmm are the mode numbers,

  • aaa and bbb are the dimensions of the membrane.

Full derivation of 2D rectangular membrane problem

To extend the solution of the 1D guitar string problem to 2D, you can analyze a 2D vibrating membrane, such as a rectangular or circular drumhead. Here is a step-by-step solution for a 2D rectangular membrane:

Solution for a 2D Rectangular Membrane

Consider a rectangular membrane with dimensions LxL_xLx​ and LyL_yLy​, fixed along its edges. The wave equation for the membrane is:

∂2u(x,y,t)∂t2=c2(∂2u(x,y,t)∂x2+∂2u(x,y,t)∂y2)\frac{\partial^2 u(x, y, t)}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \left( \frac{\partial^2 u(x, y, t)}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u(x, y, t)}{\partial y^2} \right)∂t2∂2u(x,y,t)​=c2(∂x2∂2u(x,y,t)​+∂y2∂2u(x,y,t)​)
(39)

where ccc is the wave speed.

Separation of Variables

Assume the solution can be written as a product of functions, each depending on only one variable:

u(x,y,t)=X(x)Y(y)T(t)u(x, y, t) = X(x) Y(y) T(t)u(x,y,t)=X(x)Y(y)T(t)
(40)

Substitute this into the wave equation:

d2dt2[X(x)Y(y)T(t)]=c2(d2dx2[X(x)Y(y)T(t)]+d2dy2[X(x)Y(y)T(t)])\frac{d^2}{dt^2}[X(x) Y(y) T(t)] = c^2 \left( \frac{d^2}{dx^2}[X(x) Y(y) T(t)] + \frac{d^2}{dy^2}[X(x) Y(y) T(t)] \right)dt2d2​[X(x)Y(y)T(t)]=c2(dx2d2​[X(x)Y(y)T(t)]+dy2d2​[X(x)Y(y)T(t)])
(41)

This simplifies to:

X(x)Y(y)d2T(t)dt2=c2(T(t)d2X(x)dx2Y(y)+T(t)X(x)d2Y(y)dy2)X(x) Y(y) \frac{d^2 T(t)}{dt^2} = c^2 \left( T(t) \frac{d^2 X(x)}{dx^2} Y(y) + T(t) X(x) \frac{d^2 Y(y)}{dy^2} \right)X(x)Y(y)dt2d2T(t)​=c2(T(t)dx2d2X(x)​Y(y)+T(t)X(x)dy2d2Y(y)​)
(42)

Divide through by X(x)Y(y)T(t)X(x) Y(y) T(t)X(x)Y(y)T(t):

1c21T(t)d2T(t)dt2=1X(x)d2X(x)dx2+1Y(y)d2Y(y)dy2\frac{1}{c^2} \frac{1}{T(t)} \frac{d^2 T(t)}{dt^2} = \frac{1}{X(x)} \frac{d^2 X(x)}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{Y(y)} \frac{d^2 Y(y)}{dy^2}c21​T(t)1​dt2d2T(t)​=X(x)1​dx2d2X(x)​+Y(y)1​dy2d2Y(y)​
(43)

Since the left side depends only on ttt and the right side depends only on xxx and yyy, each side must equal a constant, which we denote as −λ-\lambda−λ:

1c2d2T(t)dt2=−λ\frac{1}{c^2} \frac{d^2 T(t)}{dt^2} = -\lambdac21​dt2d2T(t)​=−λ
(44)
d2X(x)dx2+d2Y(y)dy2=−λX(x)\frac{d^2 X(x)}{dx^2} + \frac{d^2 Y(y)}{dy^2} = -\lambda X(x)dx2d2X(x)​+dy2d2Y(y)​=−λX(x)
(45)

Solving for T(t)T(t)T(t)

The ODE for T(t)T(t)T(t) is:

d2T(t)dt2+λc2T(t)=0\frac{d^2 T(t)}{dt^2} + \lambda c^2 T(t) = 0dt2d2T(t)​+λc2T(t)=0
(46)

This has the general solution:

T(t)=Acos⁡(ωt)+Bsin⁡(ωt)T(t) = A \cos(\omega t) + B \sin(\omega t)T(t)=Acos(ωt)+Bsin(ωt)
(47)

where (ω=λc)(\omega = \sqrt{\lambda} c)(ω=λ​c).

Solving for XXX and YYY

To solve the spatial part, we need to separate the problem into two parts:

d2X(x)dx2+d2Y(y)dy2=−λX(x)\frac{d^2 X(x)}{dx^2} + \frac{d^2 Y(y)}{dy^2} = -\lambda X(x)dx2d2X(x)​+dy2d2Y(y)​=−λX(x)
(48)

Assume:

d2X(x)dx2=−α2X(x)\frac{d^2 X(x)}{dx^2} = -\alpha^2 X(x)dx2d2X(x)​=−α2X(x)
(49)
d2Y(y)dy2=−(λ−α2)Y(y)\frac{d^2 Y(y)}{dy^2} = -(\lambda - \alpha^2) Y(y)dy2d2Y(y)​=−(λ−α2)Y(y)
(50)

where (λ=α2+β2)(\lambda = \alpha^2 + \beta^2)(λ=α2+β2).

So, we have:

d2X(x)dx2+α2X(x)=0\frac{d^2 X(x)}{dx^2} + \alpha^2 X(x) = 0dx2d2X(x)​+α2X(x)=0
(51)
d2Y(y)dy2+β2Y(y)=0\frac{d^2 Y(y)}{dy^2} + \beta^2 Y(y) = 0dy2d2Y(y)​+β2Y(y)=0
(52)

Boundary Conditions:

For a rectangular membrane with boundaries x=0x = 0x=0, x=Lxx = L_xx=Lx​, y=0y = 0y=0, and y=Lyy = L_yy=Ly​, we apply:

X(0)=X(Lx)=0X(0) = X(L_x) = 0X(0)=X(Lx​)=0
(53)
Y(0)=Y(Ly)=0Y(0) = Y(L_y) = 0Y(0)=Y(Ly​)=0
(54)

The solutions for X(x)X(x)X(x) and Y(y)Y(y)Y(y) are:

X(x)=Csin⁡(nπxLx)X(x) = C \sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L_x}\right)X(x)=Csin(Lx​nπx​)
(55)
Y(y)=Dsin⁡(mπyLy)Y(y) = D \sin\left(\frac{m \pi y}{L_y}\right)Y(y)=Dsin(Ly​mπy​)
(56)

where nnn and mmm are positive integers, and:

α=nπLx,β=mπLy\alpha = \frac{n \pi}{L_x}, \quad \beta = \frac{m \pi}{L_y}α=Lx​nπ​,β=Ly​mπ​
(57)

So, the general solution for u(x,y,t)u(x, y, t)u(x,y,t) is:

u(x,y,t)=∑n=1∞∑m=1∞[Anmcos⁡(ωnmt)+Bnmsin⁡(ωnmt)]sin⁡(nπxLx)sin⁡(mπyLy)u(x, y, t) = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \sum_{m=1}^\infty \left[ A_{nm} \cos\left(\omega_{nm} t\right) + B_{nm} \sin\left(\omega_{nm} t\right) \right] \sin\left(\frac{n \pi x}{L_x}\right) \sin\left(\frac{m \pi y}{L_y}\right)u(x,y,t)=n=1∑∞​m=1∑∞​[Anm​cos(ωnm​t)+Bnm​sin(ωnm​t)]sin(Lx​nπx​)sin(Ly​mπy​)
(58)

where:

ωnm=c(nπLx)2+(mπLy)2\omega_{nm} = c \sqrt{\left(\frac{n \pi}{L_x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{m \pi}{L_y}\right)^2}ωnm​=c(Lx​nπ​)2+(Ly​mπ​)2​
(59)

This solution describes the vibration modes of a 2D rectangular membrane, with each mode characterized by different integer values of nnn and mmm.

import numpy as np  
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import ipywidgets
from ipywidgets import interact, interactive, Dropdown
from matplotlib.animation import FuncAnimation
from IPython.display import HTML

import plotly.graph_objects as go
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

def membrane2d_mode(n=1, m=1, t=0):
    """
    Calculates the 2D grid of points (X, Y) and the normal mode displacement (Z) of a vibrating 
    rectangular membrane at a specific time t.

    Parameters:
    n (int): Mode number along the x-direction. Default is 1.
    m (int): Mode number along the y-direction. Default is 1.
    t (float): Time at which to evaluate the normal mode. Default is 0.

    Returns:
    X, Y, Z (numpy arrays): Grid of points in the X-Y plane and the corresponding 
                            membrane displacement Z(X, Y, t).
    """
    
    # Constants
    Lx, Ly = 1.0, 1.0  # Dimensions of the rectangular region
    v = 0.1            # Wave speed 
    omega = v * np.pi / Lx * (n**2 + m**2)  # Angular frequency for the normal mode
    
    # Create a spatial grid
    Nx, Ny = 100, 100  # Number of grid points in each dimension
    x, y = np.linspace(0, Lx, Nx), np.linspace(0, Ly, Ny)
    X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)

    # Compute the spatial part of the normal mode
    Z = np.sin(m * np.pi * X / Lx) * np.sin(n * np.pi * Y / Ly) * np.cos(omega * t)
    
    return X, Y, Z

def viz_membrane2d_plotly(n=1, m=1, t=0): 
    """
    Visualizes the 2D normal modes of a vibrating membrane on a square geometry using Plotly.
    
    This function displays both a 2D contour plot and a 3D surface plot of the membrane displacement.

    Parameters:
    n (int): Mode number along the x-direction. Default is 1.
    m (int): Mode number along the y-direction. Default is 1.
    t (float): Time at which to evaluate the normal mode. Default is 0.
    """
    
    # Get the membrane displacement at time t
    X, Y, Z = membrane2d_mode(n, m, t)
    
    # Create a Plotly subplot with 2 views: 2D contour and 3D surface
    fig = make_subplots(
        rows=1, 
        cols=2, 
        subplot_titles=('2D Contour Plot', '3D Surface Plot'),
        specs=[[{"type": "contour"}, {"type": "surface"}]]
    )
    
    # Add 2D contour plot to the left side
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Contour(x=X.flatten(), y=Y.flatten(), z=Z.flatten(), colorscale='RdBu'),
        row=1, col=1
    )

    # Add 3D surface plot to the right side
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Surface(x=X, y=Y, z=Z, colorscale='RdBu'),
        row=1, col=2
    )

    # Update the layout for better visualization
    fig.update_layout(
        title_text="2D Contour and 3D Surface Plots of Membrane Vibrational Normal Modes",
        width=1000,
        height=500
    )
    
    # Show the figure
    fig.show()

interact(viz_membrane2d_plotly, n=(1,1), m=(2,3), t=(0,100))

The sound of music.¶

  • Music produced by musical instruments is a combination of sound waves with frequencies corresponding to a superposition of the normal modes (called harmonics or overtones in music) of those instruments.

  • Learn more from this series.

Example Problems¶

Problem 1: Simple Harmonic Oscillator¶

Solve the ODE:

d2ydt2+ω2y=0\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + \omega^2 y = 0dt2d2y​+ω2y=0
(60)

where ω\omegaω is a constant.

Solution:

The characteristic equation is:

r2+ω2=0r^2 + \omega^2 = 0r2+ω2=0
(61)

Solving for rrr:

r=±iωr = \pm i \omegar=±iω
(62)

Thus, the general solution is:

y(t)=C1cos⁡(ωt)+C2sin⁡(ωt)y(t) = C_1 \cos(\omega t) + C_2 \sin(\omega t)y(t)=C1​cos(ωt)+C2​sin(ωt)
(63)

where C1C_1C1​ and C2C_2C2​ are constants determined by initial conditions.

Problem 2: Damped Oscillator¶

Solve the ODE:

d2ydt2+2βdydt+ω2y=0\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 2 \beta \frac{dy}{dt} + \omega^2 y = 0dt2d2y​+2βdtdy​+ω2y=0
(64)

where β\betaβ and ω\omegaω are constants. Consider cases of β2<ω2\beta^2 < \omega^2β2<ω2, β2>ω2\beta^2 > \omega^2β2>ω2 and β2=ω2\beta^2 = \omega^2β2=ω2

Solution:

The characteristic equation is:

r2+2βr+ω2=0r^2 + 2 \beta r + \omega^2 = 0r2+2βr+ω2=0
(65)

Solving for rrr using the quadratic formula:

r=−2β±(2β)2−4ω22r = \frac{-2 \beta \pm \sqrt{(2 \beta)^2 - 4 \omega^2}}{2}r=2−2β±(2β)2−4ω2​​
(66)
r=−β±β2−ω2r = -\beta \pm \sqrt{\beta^2 - \omega^2}r=−β±β2−ω2​
(67)

Depending on the discriminant β2−ω2\beta^2 - \omega^2β2−ω2, we have three cases:

  1. Underdamping (β2<ω2\beta^2 < \omega^2β2<ω2):

    y(t)=e−βt[C1cos⁡(ωdt)+C2sin⁡(ωdt)]y(t) = e^{-\beta t} [C_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + C_2 \sin(\omega_d t)]y(t)=e−βt[C1​cos(ωd​t)+C2​sin(ωd​t)]
    (68)

    where ωd=ω2−β2\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega^2 - \beta^2}ωd​=ω2−β2​.

  2. Critical Damping (β2=ω2\beta^2 = \omega^2β2=ω2):

    y(t)=(C1+C2t)e−βty(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t) e^{-\beta t}y(t)=(C1​+C2​t)e−βt
    (69)

  3. Overdamping (β2>ω2\beta^2 > \omega^2β2>ω2):

    y(t)=C1er1t+C2er2ty(t) = C_1 e^{r_1 t} + C_2 e^{r_2 t}y(t)=C1​er1​t+C2​er2​t
    (70)

    where r1r_1r1​ and r2r_2r2​ are the distinct real roots found from the quadratic formula.

Problem 3: ODE with Constant Coefficients¶

Solve the ODE:

d2ydt2−4y=0\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} - 4 y = 0dt2d2y​−4y=0
(71)
Solution:

The characteristic equation for this ODE is:

r2−4=0r^2 - 4 = 0r2−4=0
(72)

Solving for rrr:

r2=4r^2 = 4r2=4
(73)
r=±2r = \pm 2r=±2
(74)

Thus, the general solution is:

y(t)=C1e2t+C2e−2ty(t) = C_1 e^{2t} + C_2 e^{-2t}y(t)=C1​e2t+C2​e−2t
(75)

where C1C_1C1​ and C2C_2C2​ are constants determined by initial conditions.

Problem 4: Simple 2nd order ODE¶

Solve the ODE:

d2ydt2+9y=0\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 9 y = 0dt2d2y​+9y=0
(76)
Solution:

The characteristic equation for this ODE is:

r2+9=0r^2 + 9 = 0r2+9=0
(77)

Solving for rrr:

r2=−9r^2 = -9r2=−9
(78)
r=±3ir = \pm 3ir=±3i
(79)

Thus, the general solution is:

y(t)=C1cos⁡(3t)+C2sin⁡(3t)y(t) = C_1 \cos(3t) + C_2 \sin(3t)y(t)=C1​cos(3t)+C2​sin(3t)
(80)

where C1C_1C1​ and C2C_2C2​ are constants determined by initial conditions.

Problem 5: When ODE gives repeated roots¶

Solve the ODE:

d2ydt2−6dydt+9y=0\frac{d^2 y}{dt^2} - 6 \frac{dy}{dt} + 9 y = 0dt2d2y​−6dtdy​+9y=0
(81)
Solution:

The characteristic equation for this ODE is:

r2−6r+9=0r^2 - 6r + 9 = 0r2−6r+9=0
(82)

Factoring:

(r−3)2=0(r - 3)^2 = 0(r−3)2=0
(83)

Thus:

r=3 (repeated root)r = 3 \text{ (repeated root)}r=3 (repeated root)
(84)

The general solution for repeated roots is:

y(t)=(C1+C2t)e3ty(t) = (C_1 + C_2 t) e^{3t}y(t)=(C1​+C2​t)e3t
(85)

where C1C_1C1​ and C2C_2C2​ are constants determined by initial conditions.
